mission.title: Install and Configure MySQL Operator for Kubernetes (Oracle)
mission.description: The MySQL Operator for Kubernetes simplifies the management of MySQL InnoDB Cluster setups within Kubernetes, automating tasks such as deployment, upgrades, and backups. It is ideal for users looking to leverage MySQL in a cloud-native environment.

steps.title: Deploy Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
First, apply the Custom Resource Definitions necessary for the MySQL Operator. Use the following command:
```sh
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mysql/mysql-operator/9.0/deploy/deploy-crds.yaml
```

steps.title: Deploy MySQL Operator
steps.description:
Next, deploy the MySQL Operator using the manifest file:
```sh
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mysql/mysql-operator/9.0/deploy/deploy-operator.yaml
```
Verify that the operator is running:
```sh
kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator
```

steps.title: Create MySQL Root User Secret
steps.description:
Create a secret for the MySQL root user credentials:
```sh
kubectl create secret generic mypwds \
        --from-literal=rootUser=root \
        --from-literal=rootHost=% \
        --from-literal=rootPassword="sakila"
```

steps.title: Define MySQL InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
Create a YAML file (e.g., `mycluster.yaml`) to define your MySQL InnoDB Cluster:
```yaml
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: "8.4"
```
Then apply it:
```sh
kubectl apply -f mycluster.yaml
```

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
Set up monitoring for your MySQL cluster using Prometheus. Create a ServiceMonitor:
```yaml
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: ServiceMonitor
metadata:
  name: mysql-monitor
  labels:
    app: mysql
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mysql
  endpoints:
  - port: mysql
    interval: 30s
```

steps.title: Verify Installation
steps.description:
Check the cluster information and node status:
```sh
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get innodbclusters
```

uninstall.title: Remove MySQL Operator
uninstall.description:
To uninstall the MySQL Operator, first delete the InnoDB Cluster and then the operator and CRDs:
```sh
kubectl delete -f mycluster.yaml
kubectl delete -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mysql/mysql-operator/9.0/deploy/deploy-operator.yaml
kubectl delete -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mysql/mysql-operator/9.0/deploy/deploy-crds.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade MySQL Operator
upgrade.description:
To upgrade from version 8.4 to 9.0, first back up your data and configurations. Check the operator's current version:
```sh
kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator -o=jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[:1].image}'
```
Then apply the new operator version:
```sh
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mysql/mysql-operator/9.0/deploy/deploy-operator.yaml
```
Verify the new version is running:
```sh
kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator
```

troubleshooting.title: Operator Not Running
troubleshooting.description:
If the MySQL Operator is not running, check the logs:
```sh
kubectl logs -n mysql-operator deployment/mysql-operator
```
Ensure that your Kubernetes version is compatible and that all required CRDs are applied.

troubleshooting.title: InnoDB Cluster Not Starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the InnoDB Cluster fails to start, check the secret for correct credentials:
```sh
kubectl get secret mypwds -o yaml
```
Ensure the root password is correct and that the secret is correctly referenced in your cluster definition.

troubleshooting.title: Resource Quotas Exceeded
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter resource quota issues, check the current resource usage:
```sh
kubectl describe quota -n mysql-operator
```
Adjust your resource requests/limits in the InnoDB Cluster spec accordingly.

troubleshooting.title: Version Mismatch
troubleshooting.description: If you see errors related to version mismatches, ensure that the MySQL version specified in your InnoDB Cluster matches the operator's capabilities. Update the version in your cluster definition if necessary.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for new MySQL features and improved backup mechanisms.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for MySQL versions below 8.0.

versionNotes.changes: Initial release with basic functionalities for managing MySQL clusters.

resolution.summary: A successful setup includes a running MySQL Operator, a configured InnoDB Cluster, and verification of the cluster's health and accessibility. You should be able to manage MySQL instances through Kubernetes seamlessly.
codeSnippets: Verify that the operator is running:

apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: "8.4"

apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: ServiceMonitor
metadata:
  name: mysql-monitor
  labels:
    app: mysql
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mysql
  endpoints:
  - port: mysql
    interval: 30s

prerequisites.cloudCLI: optional cloud CLI tool (gcloud, aws, az, oci)
prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and kubectl configured to interact with it.
